title: Martin Luther King Jr. born in Atlanta
description: Civil rights leader Martin Luther King Jr. was born in Atlanta, Georgia. He would go on to lead the Montgomery Bus Boycott, the SCLC, and the 1963 March on Washington, and be jailed seventeen times before his assassination in 1968.

title: J20 inauguration mass arrests in Washington, DC
description: Police kettled and arrested 234 people protesting Donald Trump's inauguration in downtown Washington, DC, charging most under a single felony rioting indictment. The mass prosecution collapsed in 2018 after a string of acquittals and dismissals.

title: Paris Peace Accords end direct U.S. role in Vietnam War
description: The Paris Peace Accords were signed, formally ending direct U.S. military involvement in the Vietnam War. Hundreds of imprisoned U.S. draft resisters and conscientious objectors remained in federal custody, and the unconditional pardon of draft evaders would not come until 1977.

title: MLK's home in Montgomery bombed during bus boycott
description: Segregationists bombed Martin Luther King Jr.'s home on South Jackson Street in Montgomery, Alabama during the bus boycott. King's wife Coretta and infant daughter Yolanda were home but unharmed. King calmed an armed crowd that gathered outside.

title: Civil rights organizer Rosa Parks born in Alabama
description: Rosa Parks was born in Tuskegee, Alabama. Long before the 1955 Montgomery bus protest she was a trained NAACP investigator who took the testimony of Recy Taylor and other Black women survivors of white violence in the rural South.

title: Seattle General Strike begins
description: More than 65,000 workers in Seattle launched a five-day general strike — the first such strike in modern U.S. history — in solidarity with shipyard workers. The city was run by a labor strike committee; federal troops and Mayor Ole Hanson's red-scare crackdown followed.

title: W.E.B. Du Bois born in Great Barrington, Massachusetts
description: W.E.B. Du Bois — historian, sociologist, NAACP co-founder, and Pan-Africanist — was born in Massachusetts. Indicted at age 82 under the Foreign Agents Registration Act for his Stockholm Peace Appeal work, he was acquitted in 1951 but had his passport revoked until 1958.

title: Dred Scott decision issued by the U.S. Supreme Court
description: The Supreme Court ruled in Dred Scott v. Sandford that Black people were not and could not be U.S. citizens. The decision helped trigger the Civil War and remains one of the most-condemned rulings in U.S. legal history.

title: My Lai Massacre by U.S. troops in Vietnam
description: U.S. Army troops of Charlie Company massacred more than 500 unarmed villagers — mostly women, children, and elderly — at Sơn Mỹ, Vietnam. Only one soldier, Lt. William Calley, was convicted; he served three and a half years of house arrest before Nixon ordered him released.

title: Civil rights worker Viola Liuzzo murdered in Alabama
description: Viola Liuzzo, a Detroit mother who had driven to Alabama to support the Selma-to-Montgomery March, was shot dead by Klansmen on Highway 80 while ferrying marchers back to Selma. An FBI informant was in the killers' car; the Bureau then smeared her in the press.

title: LBJ announces he won't seek re-election
description: President Lyndon B. Johnson, facing mass anti-war revolt and a primary challenge from anti-war senators, announced he would not seek re-election. The decision deepened the political crisis that produced Chicago 1968 and the Days of Rage.

title: Paul Robeson born in Princeton, New Jersey
description: Singer, actor, athlete and political organizer Paul Robeson was born to a formerly enslaved father in Princeton. McCarthy-era persecution destroyed his career — he was blacklisted, denounced by HUAC, and had his passport revoked from 1950 to 1958.

title: Colfax massacre in Reconstruction Louisiana
description: A white-supremacist militia massacred 60–150 Black Republicans defending the Grant Parish, Louisiana courthouse. The Supreme Court's 1876 reversal of the federal indictments in U.S. v. Cruikshank gutted Reconstruction-era civil rights enforcement for nearly a century.

title: MLK writes 'Letter from Birmingham Jail'
description: Jailed for an Easter weekend desegregation march, Martin Luther King Jr. wrote his 'Letter from Birmingham Jail' on the margins of a smuggled newspaper. The letter became one of the foundational defenses of nonviolent civil disobedience in U.S. history.

title: Oklahoma City federal building bombing
description: Far-right anti-government extremists Timothy McVeigh and Terry Nichols bombed the Alfred P. Murrah Federal Building in Oklahoma City, killing 168 people including 19 children. McVeigh was executed in 2001; Nichols is serving life in federal prison.

title: Columbia University student strike begins
description: Students occupied five buildings at Columbia University to demand the university end work for the Pentagon and stop displacing Harlem residents to build a segregated gym. A week later NYPD raided and beat hundreds; the campus stayed shut down for the rest of the term.

title: Los Angeles uprising after Rodney King verdict
description: Hours after a Simi Valley jury acquitted four LAPD officers in the videotaped beating of Rodney King, Los Angeles erupted in the largest U.S. urban uprising in decades. Six days of revolt left 63 dead, 2,000+ injured, and over 12,000 arrested.

title: John Scopes arrested for teaching evolution
description: Tennessee schoolteacher John Scopes was arrested under the Butler Act for teaching evolution. The ACLU's defense — argued by Clarence Darrow against William Jennings Bryan — became the first nationally broadcast trial in U.S. history.

title: Philadelphia police bomb MOVE house, killing eleven
description: Philadelphia police dropped a satchel bomb on the roof of the MOVE family home on Osage Avenue. The fire killed eleven people, including five children, and burned 61 row houses to the ground. No official was ever criminally prosecuted.

title: George Floyd murdered by Minneapolis police
description: Minneapolis officer Derek Chauvin knelt on George Floyd's neck for 9 minutes 29 seconds, killing him. The bystander video sparked the largest protest wave in U.S. history, with an estimated 15–26 million people in the streets across all 50 states by mid-June.

title: Watergate burglars' first break-in at DNC headquarters
description: G. Gordon Liddy, James McCord and the Cuban exiles broke into the Democratic National Committee offices at the Watergate complex for the first time, installing wiretaps. The second break-in to fix the bugs three weeks later would unravel the Nixon presidency.

title: Robert F. Kennedy shot in Los Angeles
description: Senator Robert F. Kennedy was shot at the Ambassador Hotel in Los Angeles minutes after winning the California Democratic primary on an anti-war platform. He died the following day. Sirhan Sirhan was convicted; the case continues to draw scrutiny.

title: Watergate burglars arrested at DNC headquarters
description: Five men were arrested inside the Democratic National Committee offices at the Watergate complex in Washington, DC, with wiretapping equipment. The cover-up over the next two years would force the only U.S. presidential resignation.

title: Mississippi Freedom Summer workers go missing
description: Civil rights workers James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner disappeared in Neshoba County, Mississippi after being released from a sheriff's lockup. Their bodies were found six weeks later in an earthen dam. The Klan and county deputies had killed them.

title: Frederick Douglass's 'What to the Slave is the Fourth of July?'
description: At an Independence Day event in Rochester, NY, Frederick Douglass delivered one of the most blistering speeches in U.S. history: 'This Fourth of July is yours, not mine. You may rejoice; I must mourn.' The speech indicted the hypocrisy of a slave republic celebrating liberty.

title: FALN clemency campaign opens with Carter remarks
description: Former President Jimmy Carter publicly endorsed the campaign to release the Puerto Rican FALN political prisoners. The campaign would culminate fourteen years later with President Clinton's 1999 clemency for 16 of the prisoners.

title: Black Panther David Hilliard charged with threatening Nixon
description: Black Panther chief of staff David Hilliard was indicted for threatening President Nixon's life in a Moratorium Day anti-war speech in San Francisco. The charge was a felony carrying a five-year sentence. The case was eventually dismissed on First Amendment grounds.

title: Cuban revolutionaries attack Moncada Barracks
description: Fidel Castro and 160 fighters attacked the Moncada Barracks at Santiago de Cuba, launching the Cuban Revolution. The attack failed; Castro was tried, gave his 'History Will Absolve Me' speech in court, and was sentenced to 15 years on the Isle of Pines.

title: Hoover's troops attack Bonus Army encampment
description: Under President Hoover's orders, U.S. Army troops commanded by Douglas MacArthur attacked the Bonus Army — WWI veterans camped in Washington demanding promised bonus payments. Cavalry, tear gas, and tanks burned the camp; at least two veterans and one infant died.

title: Bodies of Chaney, Goodman, and Schwerner recovered in Mississippi
description: The bodies of civil rights workers James Chaney, Andrew Goodman, and Michael Schwerner were recovered from an earthen dam in Neshoba County, Mississippi, six weeks after their abduction and murder by Klansmen and county deputies during Freedom Summer.

title: United States drops atomic bomb on Hiroshima
description: The United States dropped the 'Little Boy' uranium bomb on Hiroshima, killing an estimated 140,000 people by year's end. The bombing — and Nagasaki three days later — became the founding wound of the postwar anti-nuclear and Plowshares movements.

title: Richard Nixon resigns the U.S. presidency
description: Facing certain impeachment over Watergate, President Richard Nixon became the only U.S. president to resign. Gerald Ford pardoned him a month later, foreclosing any criminal accountability for the Watergate burglary cover-up.

title: Heather Heyer killed at Charlottesville white supremacist rally
description: Neo-Nazi James Alex Fields Jr. drove his car into a crowd of counter-protesters at the 'Unite the Right' rally in Charlottesville, Virginia, killing 32-year-old paralegal and anti-racist Heather Heyer. Fields was convicted of first-degree murder and federal hate crimes.

title: 19th Amendment ratified, women win the vote
description: Tennessee became the 36th state to ratify the 19th Amendment, granting women the right to vote in the United States — though Black women and women of color in much of the country would not gain meaningful access to the ballot for another 45 years.

title: Frederick Douglass escapes slavery
description: Frederick Douglass, then 20 years old, escaped enslavement in Maryland by boarding a train north dressed as a free Black sailor with borrowed seaman's papers. He would become the most prominent Black abolitionist of the 19th century.

title: Christiana Resistance against the Fugitive Slave Act
description: Free Black people and fugitive slaves in Christiana, Pennsylvania armed themselves and successfully resisted slaveholder Edward Gorsuch's attempt to seize escaped people under the 1850 Fugitive Slave Act. Gorsuch was killed; thirty-eight people were tried for treason and acquitted.

title: U.S.-backed coup overthrows Salvador Allende in Chile
description: General Augusto Pinochet's CIA-backed coup overthrew the elected socialist government of Salvador Allende in Chile. Allende died in the presidential palace; tens of thousands were tortured and disappeared in the 17-year dictatorship that followed.

title: Attica prison uprising ends with state assault, 43 killed
description: After four days of negotiations, New York Governor Nelson Rockefeller ordered state troopers to retake Attica prison by force. The assault killed 39 people — 29 prisoners and 10 hostages, all shot by state officers — and 4 more during the uprising itself.

title: Occupy Wall Street begins in Zuccotti Park
description: Several hundred people set up an encampment at Zuccotti Park in Manhattan's Financial District, launching Occupy Wall Street. By November the movement had spread to 951 cities in 82 countries before NYPD cleared the park in a coordinated nationwide eviction wave.

title: Tlatelolco massacre of student protesters in Mexico City
description: Ten days before the Olympics opened, Mexican military and paramilitary forces opened fire on a peaceful student rally in Mexico City's Tlatelolco square. Estimates of the dead range from 300 to over 1,000. The massacre haunted the Mexican state for decades.

title: Che Guevara executed in Bolivia
description: Revolutionary Ernesto 'Che' Guevara was executed by Bolivian soldiers under CIA direction in La Higuera, Bolivia, a day after being captured. His remains were hidden for nearly 30 years before being exhumed and returned to Cuba in 1997.

title: MLK awarded Nobel Peace Prize
description: Martin Luther King Jr. was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize at age 35, the youngest recipient at the time. The FBI's COINTELPRO had been at work for over a year trying to discredit him, and would the next month send him an anonymous letter urging him to kill himself.

title: March on the Pentagon, 100,000 anti-war protesters
description: 100,000 people marched on the Pentagon to demand the end of the Vietnam War; 50,000 surrounded the building, and Norman Mailer, Noam Chomsky, and 681 others were arrested. Allen Ginsberg led an exorcism of the Pentagon. The protest is often dated as the symbolic start of the movement's mass phase.

title: USA PATRIOT Act signed into law
description: Less than seven weeks after September 11, President George W. Bush signed the USA PATRIOT Act. The law dramatically expanded warrantless surveillance, federal material-support statutes, and the FBI's authority to investigate political organizations — tools that have since been used against Muslim, anti-war, and Black liberation organizers.

title: U.S. embassy hostages taken in Tehran
description: Iranian students seized the U.S. embassy in Tehran and took 52 American diplomatic personnel hostage. The 444-day crisis ended the day Reagan was inaugurated and ushered in four decades of U.S.-Iran hostility — and the federal targeting of Iranian Americans and anti-shah dissidents at home.

title: Jonestown mass murder-suicide in Guyana
description: More than 900 members of the Peoples Temple died at Jonestown, Guyana, in a forced mass murder-suicide engineered by founder Jim Jones, hours after he ordered the assassination of U.S. Rep. Leo Ryan and a journalist on the Port Kaituma airstrip.

title: President John F. Kennedy assassinated in Dallas
description: President John F. Kennedy was assassinated as his motorcade rolled through Dallas. Lee Harvey Oswald was arrested ninety minutes later and killed two days later by Jack Ruby in the basement of Dallas police headquarters.

title: Rosa Parks arrested in Montgomery
description: Rosa Parks, NAACP secretary and trained civil rights organizer, was arrested in Montgomery, Alabama for refusing to give up her bus seat to a white passenger. Her arrest triggered the 381-day Montgomery Bus Boycott that launched the modern civil rights movement.

title: Montgomery Bus Boycott begins
description: Four days after Rosa Parks's arrest, Montgomery's Black community launched a one-day bus boycott that became a 381-day refusal. The Montgomery Improvement Association elected a 26-year-old Baptist minister, Martin Luther King Jr., as its president.

title: 13th Amendment abolishing slavery ratified
description: Georgia became the 27th state to ratify the 13th Amendment, formally abolishing slavery in the United States — 'except as a punishment for crime,' a clause that has structured the convict-leasing, chain-gang, and modern prison-labor systems ever since.

title: Modoc War prisoner Captain Jack hanged in Oregon
description: Kintpuash, the Modoc leader known as Captain Jack, was hanged at Fort Klamath, Oregon along with three other Modoc prisoners after a U.S. military tribunal convicted them of war crimes for the killing of General Edward Canby during the Modoc War.

title: Mumia Abu-Jamal arrested in Philadelphia
description: Journalist and former Black Panther Mumia Abu-Jamal was arrested at the scene of the shooting death of Philadelphia police officer Daniel Faulkner. Tried in 1982 and sentenced to death by a jury from which Black jurors had been systematically struck, Mumia remains incarcerated in Pennsylvania.

title: George Washington dies — 317 enslaved people on Mount Vernon
description: George Washington died at Mount Vernon. His will freed the 123 people he personally enslaved on his death; the 194 others on the estate, owned by his wife Martha's first family, remained enslaved. Washington's slave ledger is among the most-documented in U.S. history.

title: Reality Winner sentenced to 63 months for NSA leak
description: Air Force veteran and NSA contractor Reality Winner was sentenced to 63 months in federal prison — the longest sentence ever imposed under the Espionage Act for an unauthorized release of information to the press, for leaking a single document on Russian interference in the 2016 election.

title: 38 Dakota men hanged in Mankato — largest mass execution in U.S. history
description: Thirty-eight Dakota Sioux men were hanged simultaneously at Mankato, Minnesota, after President Lincoln approved their executions for participation in the U.S.–Dakota War. It remains the largest mass execution in U.S. history. Lincoln had commuted 264 other death sentences.
